final_output.final_deliverable:
Answers to the six Wikipedia page questions (each with verbatim evidence quoted from the visited page and the citation URL):

1) Axolotl — What is the scientific name of the axolotl?
Answer: Ambystoma mexicanum
Citation: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Axolotl
Verbatim quote from the page:
"Binomial name
Ambystoma mexicanum
(Shaw and Nodder, 1798)"

2) Voynich manuscript — In which library is the Voynich manuscript currently held?
Answer: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library (Yale University)
Citation: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voynich_manuscript
Verbatim quote from the page:
"Since 1969, it has been held in Yale University's Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library."

3) Pando clonal colony — In which US state is the Pando clonal colony located?
Answer: Utah
Citation: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pando_(tree)
Verbatim quote from the page:
"Pando (from Latin pando 'I spread') is the name of a quaking aspen (Populus tremuloides) clone located in Sevier County, Utah, United States, in the Fishlake National Forest."

4) Antikythera mechanism — Approximately what century BCE was the Antikythera mechanism built?
Answer: 2nd century BC (approximate)
Citation: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antikythera_mechanism
Verbatim quote from the page:
"Created
2nd century BC"
(also relevant supporting excerpts on dating: "The instrument is believed to have been designed and constructed by Hellenistic scientists and been variously dated to about 87 BC, between 150 and 100 BC, or 205 BC.")

5) Mantis shrimp vision — How many types of photoreceptor cells do mantis shrimp have (approximate)?
Answer: Between 12 and 16 types of photoreceptor cells (approximate)
Citation: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mantis_shrimp
Verbatim quote from the page:
"Compared with the four types of photoreceptor cell that humans possess in their eyes, the eyes of a mantis shrimp have between 12 and 16 types of photoreceptor cells."

6) Svalbard Global Seed Vault — In which country is the Svalbard Global Seed Vault located?
Answer: Norway
Citation: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Svalbard_Global_Seed_Vault
Verbatim quote from the page:
"The Svalbard Global Seed Vault (Norwegian: Svalbard globale fr�f8hvelv) is a secure backup facility for the world's crop diversity on the Norwegian island of Spitsbergen in the remote Arctic Svalbard archipelago."
final_output.action_summary: Provided six direct answers (scientific name, library holding, US state, century BCE, photoreceptor count range, country) with verbatim quotations from the corresponding Wikipedia pages and the citation URL for each.
